front: Procurement requests: RFI vs RFQ vs RFP vs IFB — when do you use each?

legend.meaning: Request for Information — early; gauge the market and supplier capabilities (not a purchase)
Request for Quotation — price-driven; standard / commodity items
Request for Proposal — solution-driven; complex needs, sellers propose how + price
Invitation for Bid (a.k.a. RFB) — detailed sealed bids; award the lowest compliant price

back: Think information → price → solution. RFI just gathers information. RFQ and IFB compete on price for well-defined work. RFP invites proposed solutions when the work is complex.
tip: Trap: RFC is NOT a procurement document — in PMP it means Request for Change. Don't let the 'RF' prefix fool you.

front: Contract types: Fixed-Price vs Cost-Reimbursable vs T&M — who carries the risk?

legend.meaning: Fixed-Price family — seller carries the cost risk; best when scope is well defined
Cost-Reimbursable family — buyer carries the cost risk; for uncertain scope
Time & Materials — hybrid; good for staff augmentation or short, unclear scope

back: Cost risk shifts from the seller (fixed price) to the buyer (cost-reimbursable), with T&M in between. The clearer the scope, the more you can fix the price.
tip: FFP = firm fixed price · FPIF = + incentive fee · FP-EPA = + economic price adjustment. CPFF / CPIF / CPAF = cost plus fixed / incentive / award fee.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='44' y='24' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>less buyer risk</text><text x='276' y='24' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>more buyer risk</text><line x1='44' y1='34' x2='276' y2='34' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><polygon points='276,30 285,34 276,38' fill='currentColor'/><rect x='40' y='50' width='92' height='26' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='132' y='50' width='68' height='26' fill='#F5ECD8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='200' y='50' width='84' height='26' fill='#F6E4DF' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><text x='86' y='67' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Fixed price</text><text x='166' y='67' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>T&M</text><text x='242' y='67' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Cost-reimb.</text><text x='86' y='94' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>seller carries risk</text><text x='242' y='94' fill='currentColor' font-size='8.5' text-anchor='middle'>buyer carries risk</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cost risk moves from seller (fixed price) to buyer (cost-reimbursable).

front: OPA vs EEF — what's the difference?

legend.meaning: Organizational Process Assets — your org's processes, templates, lessons learned, knowledge bases
Enterprise Environmental Factors — conditions outside the team's control (culture, market, regulations, systems)

back: OPAs are things the organization HAS that help you — you can use and update them. EEFs are conditions you must work WITHIN and generally can't change.
tip: Lessons learned, templates, processes = OPA. Government regulations, market conditions, org culture = EEF.

legend.meaning: For KNOWN risks (identified). Inside the cost baseline. The PM can authorise its use.
For UNKNOWN risks (unforeseen). Outside the cost baseline. Management must authorise its use.

back: Known-unknowns get a contingency reserve held inside the baseline; unknown-unknowns get a management reserve held above the baseline.
tip: Contingency = inside the baseline, PM controls. Management = outside the baseline, needs management approval.

front: Breakdown structures: WBS vs OBS vs RBS vs RAM?

legend.meaning: Work Breakdown Structure — decomposition of the project SCOPE (lowest level = work package)
Organizational Breakdown Structure — work arranged by org unit / department
Risk Breakdown Structure — risks by category (also: Resource Breakdown Structure!)
Responsibility Assignment Matrix — who does what, e.g. a RACI chart

back: WBS = scope, OBS = org units, RBS = risk (or resource) categories, RAM = responsibilities.
tip: RBS is itself confusable: Risk Breakdown Structure vs Resource Breakdown Structure — read the context.

legend.meaning: Quality Assurance — PROCESS focused; prevention; 'are we using the right processes?' (Manage Quality)
Quality Control — PRODUCT focused; inspection; 'is the deliverable correct?' (Control Quality)
Cost of Quality — total cost of prevention, appraisal and failure

back: QA improves and assures the processes (proactive, prevention). QC inspects the actual deliverables (reactive, detection).
tip: QA = process / prevention / Manage Quality. QC = product / inspection / Control Quality.

front: Estimate accuracy: ROM vs Budget vs Definitive?

legend.meaning: Rough Order of Magnitude — early, −25% to +75%
Budget estimate — −10% to +25%
Definitive estimate — late & detailed, −5% to +10%

back: Estimates get tighter as the project progresses and detail grows — from a wide ROM early to a narrow definitive estimate later.
tip: ROM is the widest (−25/+75); definitive is the tightest (−5/+10). Ranges narrow over time.

front: Change terms: CR vs RFC vs CCB vs ICC?

legend.meaning: Change Request — the formal request to change something
Request for Change — another name for a change request
Change Control Board — the group that approves or rejects changes
Integrated Change Control — the process of reviewing and deciding changes

back: A change request (CR) is the request; the change control board (CCB) decides; integrated change control is the process that runs it. ('RFC' / 'Request for Change' is an ITIL term, not PMI.)
tip: In PMI the term is Change Request (CR). 'RFC' (Request for Change) is ITIL, not PMI — don't expect it on the exam.

front: PMO types: Supportive vs Controlling vs Directive?

legend.meaning: Low control — provides templates, training and lessons learned (consultative)
Moderate control — requires compliance with frameworks / standards
High control — directly manages the projects

back: Authority rises Supportive → Controlling → Directive. Supportive helps, Controlling requires, Directive runs the projects.
tip: Degree of control is the differentiator: support < compliance < direct management.

legend.meaning: Delay an activity without delaying the EARLY START of any successor
Delay an activity without delaying the PROJECT end date

back: Total float is slack against the project finish; free float is slack against the next activity. Critical-path activities have zero total float.
tip: Free = relative to the next activity; Total = relative to the project end.
